title: Nokedli

overview.description: Soft, irregular Hungarian egg noodles made from a simple batter and traditionally served with dishes such as Chicken Paprikash.

method: Bring a large saucepan of salted water to a gentle boil.
Mix the flour and salt in a bowl.
Beat the eggs and add them to the flour.
Gradually add enough water to make a thick, sticky batter.
Beat the batter vigorously with a wooden spoon for a minute or two until smooth and elastic.
Working in batches, push the batter through a nokedli maker or a large-holed colander directly into the simmering water.
Cook until the noodles float to the surface, then continue cooking for approximately 1–2 minutes.
Remove with a slotted spoon and drain.
Toss with a little butter to prevent sticking.

learning.did-you-know: Nokedli are also known as galuska in some parts of Hungary and neighbouring Central European countries.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How a simple egg batter can be turned into soft, irregular noodles without rolling or cutting dough.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple traditional accompaniment that is worth learning because it turns a dish like Chicken Paprikash into a complete Hungarian meal.
chapman-tips: The batter should be thick and sticky rather than pourable.
Don't worry about making every piece the same size. The irregular shapes are part of the character of nokedli.
Cook in batches so the water does not cool too much.
Toss the cooked noodles with butter immediately to stop them sticking together.
If using a colander, work carefully and keep your hands away from the boiling water.
notes.batch-cooking: Best made fresh. Cooked nokedli can be refrigerated, but the texture is better when freshly made.
notes.leftovers: Leftover nokedli can be reheated in a frying pan with butter or added to soups and stews.
